claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 3 children:
  - Alex is 21 years old and does not live with you.
  - Blake is 15 years old and does not live with you.
  - Charlie is 11 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 21, which is 20 or over
  - Claimant contributes £50.00/week towards child's upkeep (>= £26.05) and no other claimaint has priority
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child works 24+ hours/week and is not in approved education
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Blake:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 15 (under 16)
  - Child does not live with claimant and weekly upkeep (£10.00) is below the Child Benefit rate (£26.05)
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Charlie:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 11 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.reason: Child is 21, which is 20 or over; Child works 24+ hours/week and is not in approved education

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. To assess your eligibility, I need to ask you some questions about your circumstances.

First, let me start with: **Do you have any children, and if so, how many children are you inquiring about?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hello! Yes, I have three children that I'd like to ask about - Alex, Blake, and Charlie.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! I'll help you determine eligibility for Alex, Blake, and Charlie.

Let me start by asking: **How old are Alex, Blake, and Charlie?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Alex is 21, Blake is 15, and Charlie is 11.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. 

Now, let me ask about Alex first, since at 21 they are over the usual age limit. **Is Alex currently in approved education or training?** (Child Benefit can continue for children under 20 if they're in approved education or training, but Alex being 21 would generally be outside the age range.)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Alex isn't in any education or training. They actually work full-time.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying about Alex.

Now let me ask about Blake and Charlie. **Do Blake and Charlie both live with you?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Charlie lives with me, but Blake doesn't live with me.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information.

Since Blake doesn't live with you, I need to understand more about your relationship with them. **Are you making any financial contributions towards Blake's upkeep (such as for food, clothes, pocket money, or accommodation), and if so, how much do you contribute?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I do help out a bit with Blake - I contribute about £10 a week towards their upkeep.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information.

Now I need to ask: **Do you live in the UK?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you. Just a couple more questions to complete the assessment.

**Is Charlie working 24 hours or more per week, or receiving any benefits in their own right (such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance)?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Charlie isn't working at all and doesn't receive any benefits.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you. One final question: **Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for Blake or Charlie?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for them.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
## Assessment Complete

Based on the information you've provided, here's the outcome for each of your children:

**Alex (21 years old):** ❌ **Not Eligible**
- Alex is 21 and not in approved education or training. Child Benefit only covers children under 16, or under 20 if they're in approved education or training. Since Alex is working full-time and is over 20, they don't qualify.

**Blake (15 years old):** ❌ **Not Eligible**
- While Blake is under 16, they don't live with you. To qualify for Child Benefit when a child lives elsewhere, you need to contribute at least as much as the Child Benefit payment itself towards their upkeep. Your contribution of £10 per week is below the Child Benefit threshold (approximately £25.60 for the eldest child or £16.95 for additional children), so unfortunately you don't meet the requirement to be considered 'responsible' for Blake under these rules.

**Charlie (11 years old):** ✅ **Eligible**
- Charlie is under 16, lives with you, isn't working 24+ hours per week, and isn't claiming benefits in their own right. You meet all the criteria for Child Benefit for Charlie.

**Summary:** You can claim Child Benefit for Charlie. If you wanted to claim for Blake, you would need to increase your contributions to at least match the Child Benefit rate.

reasonings: Alex is 21 years old and not in approved education or training. Child Benefit is available for children under 16, or under 20 if they are in approved education or training. Since Alex is 21 and working full-time, they fall outside the age eligibility criteria for Child Benefit.

Blake is 15 years old (under 16) but does not live with you. While you are contributing £10 per week towards Blake's upkeep, to qualify for Child Benefit when a child lives elsewhere, you must contribute at least the same amount as the Child Benefit payment. The current Child Benefit rate is approximately £25.60 per week for the eldest child and £16.95 per week for additional children. Your contribution of £10 per week is less than the Child Benefit amount, so you do not meet the threshold to be considered 'responsible' for Blake under the Child Benefit rules.

Charlie is 11 years old (under 16), lives with you, is not working 24 hours or more per week, and is not receiving benefits in their own right. You meet the responsibility test as Charlie lives with you. No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Charlie. Therefore, you qualify for Child Benefit for Charlie.
arguments.overall_reasoning: You have three children: Alex (21), Blake (15), and Charlie (11). You are eligible for Child Benefit for Charlie only. Alex is too old and not in approved education/training. Blake doesn't live with you, and your £10 weekly contribution is below the Child Benefit threshold needed to establish responsibility for a child living elsewhere. Charlie lives with you and meets all eligibility criteria.

Since Alex is 21 and working full-time, they fall outside the age eligibility criteria for Child Benefit.

Blake is 15 years old (under 16) but does not live with you. The current Child Benefit rate is approximately £25.60 per week for the eldest child and £16.95 per week for additional children. Your contribution of £10 per week is less than the Child Benefit amount, so you do not meet the threshold to be considered 'responsible' for Blake under the Child Benefit rules.

Charlie is 11 years old (under 16), lives with you, is not working 24 hours or more per week, and is not receiving benefits in their own right. You meet the responsibility test as Charlie lives with you. No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Charlie. Therefore, you qualify for Child Benefit for Charlie.

Alex is too old and not in approved education/training. Blake doesn't live with you, and your £10 weekly contribution is below the Child Benefit threshold needed to establish responsibility for a child living elsewhere. Charlie lives with you and meets all eligibility criteria.
